summary: When a pre-commit hook fails, create a NEW commit after fixing - never amend the previous one
problem: Pre-commit hook rejects your commit, you fix the issue and run `git commit --amend`, which modifies the last good commit instead of creating a new one for your actual change
solution: After a hook failure, the commit did NOT happen - there is nothing to amend. Fix the issue, re-stage your files, and run `git commit` fresh with a new message
root_cause: A failed commit leaves the working tree dirty and the HEAD unchanged. --amend in this state rewrites the previous successful commit, potentially destroying its changes
